(B) easily forms anhydride on heating. Identify the compound (A).
question: Compound (A), <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>8</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>9</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi></math>, gives a white precipitate when warmed with alcoholic <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math><sub>.</sub> Oxidation of (A) gives an acid (B), <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>8</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math>. (B) easily forms anhydride on heating. Identify the compound (A).
solution: Compound (A) gives a white precipitate when warmed with alcoholic AgNO3means it contain a removable chloride atom(Benzylic position). Oxidation of (A) gives an acid (B), C8H6O4. (B) easily forms anhydride on heating. Means both acid should be ortho position of each other.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The descending order of acidity among the following compounds is:
A. Phenol
B. $4$-nitrophenol
C. $4$-methoxyphenol
D. $4$-nitrobenzoic acid
E. Benzoic acid

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
The descending order of acidity among the following compounds is:
A. Phenol
B. $4$-nitrophenol
C. $4$-methoxyphenol
D. $4$-nitrobenzoic acid
E. Benzoic acid

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Carboxylic acids are stronger acids than phenols because the carboxylate anion is stabilized by equivalent resonance structures. Thus, D and E are more acidic than A, B, and C.

For the carboxylic acids, the $-NO_2$ group in D is an electron-withdrawing group ($-I, -R$), which stabilizes the conjugate base, making D more acidic than E.

For the phenols, electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity while electron-donating groups decrease it. The $-NO_2$ group in B is electron-withdrawing, making it more acidic than A. The $-OCH_3$ group in C is electron-donating ($+R > -I$), making it less acidic than A. Thus, the order for phenols is B $>$ A $>$ C.

Combining these, the overall descending order of acidity is D $>$ E $>$ B $>$ A $>$ C.

Answer: $D>E>B>A>C$

The compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mfenced></math> is:
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mfenced></math> on treatment with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Br</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mi>FeBr</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CCl</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> produced a single isomer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>8</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>7</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>Br</mi></math> wile heating <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mfenced></math> with sodalime gives toluene. The compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
Isomer C8H7O2Br on heating with sodalime gives toluene,hence it is an acid.Moreover since it has Degree of unsaturation 5 therefore it is an aromatic carboxylic acid.Since Carboxylic acid on heating gives toluene ,therefore it is having -CH3 group attached to the benzene ring.

Now it forms single isomer on halogenation, therefore -CH3 group must be attached at para position with respect to -COOH group.

Hence correct option is 1.

subject: Chemistry
q: In Friedel-Crafts alkylation of aniline, one gets:
question: In Friedel-Crafts alkylation of aniline, one gets:
options: alkylated product with ortho and para substitution.
positively charged nitrogen at benzene ring.
solution:
Aniline does not undergo Friedel-crafts reaction due to salt formation with aluminium chloride, the Lewis acid, which is used as a catalyst, whereas aniline is a strong base. So, an acid-base reaction will take place. Thus, aniline reacts with AlCl3 to form a salt.

Due to the positive charge on the nitrogen atom (shows -I effect and it is meta directing), electrophilic substitution in the benzene ring is deactivated. Hence, the Friedel-Crafts reaction will not take place in aniline.

Compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>' also decolorises alkaline <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math>. The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math> bond/s  present in the compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>' is _____ .
question: A trisubstituted compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>', <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>12</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> gives neutral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>FeCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> test positive. Treatment of compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>' with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>Br</mi></math> gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>11</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>14</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, with hydroiodic acid gives methyl iodide and with hot conc. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> gives a compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>12</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>' also decolorises alkaline <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math>. The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math> bond/s  present in the compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>' is _____ .
solution:
The trisubstituted compound 'A', C10H12O2 gives neutral FeCl3 test positive, means the compound is phenol. Phenol on treatment with NaOH and CH3Br gives C10H11O2CH3. The given compound is reacting with hot conc. NaOH gives a compound B, C10H12O2. The reaction is Cannizzaro's reaction. Hence, compound must be benzaldehyde derivative. Compound 'A' also decolourises alkaline KMnO4, means compound contains an alkyl group attached to benzene ring. Hence, the structure of the compound can be written as follows,

q: Which of the following compounds is least likely to give effervescence of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ in presence of aq. $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ ?
question: Which of the following compounds is least likely to give effervescence of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ in presence of aq. $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ ?
solution:
Concept - Those compounds which are more acidic than $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$ can gives effervescence of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ with aq. $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$.
Release $\mathrm{CO}_2 \uparrow$ gas with aq. NaHCO 3
$\Rightarrow[\text { A.S. }]_{\text {Comp. }} \gt [\text { A.S. }]_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3}$
$\rightarrow$ Option 1,2 and 3 gives effervescence of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ gas with $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$
$\rightarrow$ Option (4) Not gives $\mathrm{CO}_2$ gas with $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$.
